featuretablefilter 0.99.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • featuretablefilter Tutorial: Complete Guide to Microbiome Feature Table Filtering
  • Changelog

Compute scree/saturation diagnostics for filtering thresholds

Source: R/compute_scree.R
compute_scree.Rd

Evaluates feature and sample retention across a gradient of thresholds to help users visualize how quickly their data collapses and choose an optimal cutoff. This is particularly useful for MAD/IQR multiplier sweeps, coverage target sweeps (Good's or Chao's), absolute count thresholds, or relative abundance cutoffs.

compute_scree(
  table,
  type = c("mad_multiplier", "iqr_multiplier", "good_coverage", "chao_coverage",
    "singleton_ratio", "cross_talk", "absolute_feature", "relative_feature", "custom"),
  thresholds = NULL,
  n_steps = 20,
  min_samples = 1,
  count_type = "both",
  verbose = TRUE
)

Arguments

table

A feature table (data.frame or matrix) with features as rows and samples as columns. First column should be feature IDs, remaining columns are sample counts.

type

Type of threshold sweep to perform:

  • "mad_multiplier" - Sweep MAD multipliers (e.g., 1 to 5 for coverage filtering)

  • "iqr_multiplier" - Sweep IQR multipliers (e.g., 0.5 to 3 for Tukey fence-based filtering)

  • "good_coverage" - Sweep Good's coverage targets (0.70 to 0.99)

  • "chao_coverage" - Sweep Chao's coverage targets (0.70 to 0.99)

  • "singleton_ratio" - Sweep singleton ratio thresholds (0.01 to 0.30 for PCR artifact detection)

  • "cross_talk" - Sweep cross-talk relative thresholds (0.0001 to 0.01 for index hopping)

  • "absolute_feature" - Sweep absolute feature abundance thresholds

  • "relative_feature" - Sweep relative abundance thresholds (0.00001 to 0.01)

  • "custom" - Use custom threshold values provided in thresholds

thresholds

For type = "custom", a numeric vector of threshold values to evaluate. Ignored for other types (generated automatically).

n_steps

Number of threshold steps to evaluate. Default is 20. More steps give smoother curves.

min_samples

Minimum number of samples required for feature retention (passed to filter_features_by_abundance). Default is 1.

count_type

Type of low-count features for singleton ratio filtering: "singleton", "doubleton", or "both". Default is "both".

verbose

Logical. Print progress information. Default is TRUE.

Value

A list containing:

results

data.frame with columns: threshold, n_features_retained, pct_features_retained, n_samples_retained, pct_samples_retained, n_reads_retained, pct_reads_retained, sparsity, collapse_rate

summary

list with key statistics including elbow points and saturation metrics

type

the type of sweep performed

parameters

list of parameters used

Details

The function generates diagnostic curves showing: - Feature retention rate (% features kept at each threshold) - Sample retention rate (% samples kept at each threshold) - Total read retention (% reads preserved) - Sparsity changes (proportion of zeros in the table) - "Collapse rate" - derivative showing where rapid losses occur

Examples

data(example_feature_table)
result <- compute_scree(example_feature_table, type = "absolute_feature",
                        n_steps = 5, verbose = FALSE)
head(result$results)
#>   threshold n_features_retained pct_features_retained n_samples_retained
#> 1         1                  49                    98                 20
#> 2         2                  45                    90                 20
#> 3         3                  44                    88                 20
#> 4       231                  26                    52                 20
#> 5     26789                   1                     2                 20
#>   pct_samples_retained n_reads_retained pct_reads_retained   sparsity
#> 1                  100          1579475          100.00000 0.05918367
#> 2                  100          1579453           99.99861 0.00000000
#> 3                  100          1579420           99.99652 0.00000000
#> 4                  100          1563515           98.98954 0.00000000
#> 5                  100           423269           26.79808 0.00000000
#>   collapse_rate
#> 1            NA
#> 2   8.000000000
#> 3   2.000000000
#> 4   0.157894737
#> 5   0.001882672
